segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The first bell tolled like a struck rib: copper and cold. Millbrook answered.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Buckets thumped into hands. Barrels rolled to the well. Children were shooed under cellar hatches that smelled of earth, turnips, and fear. On the lane, chalk marks glowed faint in lantern light -- Serana's secular ward-lines. Here hold. Here yield.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Fall back to square at second bell. The guard captain's voice ran along the wall:

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Steady on -- by files -- mind your spacing."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They listened because of the charter-sigil on Serana's cloak and because Vale said

segments.speaker: unknown
segments.text: "Do it,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: and Vale had been telling them what to do for twelve winters and they were still alive.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale moved along the palisade line the way water moves through a lock -- steady, purposeful, adjusting everything he touched.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd been Millbrook's constable since before most of these soldiers had started shaving, and he wore the years the way a good saddle wears -- shaped to the work, creased at the stress points, holding together because the alternative was unacceptable.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Broad shoulders that had narrowed with age but not with doubt. Hands that checked a spear-butt's angle by touch and corrected it before the holder knew what was wrong.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stopped at a bucket line and moved a barrel six inches to the left. Nobody asked why.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Three minutes later, a running messenger would have tripped over it in the original position, and the fact that Vale had seen the problem before it existed was so ordinary to Millbrook that nobody remarked on it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Wind carried the wrong smell: damp stone, old iron, something like cold breath blown through a keyhole. Then the first of the shadow-thralls stepped out from behind the butcher's, bending wrong at the knee.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Hold until I call,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana said, shield lifting, voice level. Her light flickered -- she made it steady with will alone, the way you make a fist when your hand wants to shake.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale moved along the line like a man checking knots before a storm.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Spears up. Eyes forward. When the paladin calls, you move. Not before."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stopped at a boy of perhaps sixteen -- spear too long, knuckles white on the haft. The boy's breathing was wrong: fast, shallow, the panic rhythm that makes you deaf to orders and blind to openings. Vale didn't grab his shoulders. Didn't shout.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He reached over and squared the spear angle with one hand, the way a father straightens a collar, and the touch itself was the calming -- the casual competence of a man who'd done this before and planned to do it again.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "You showed up,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale said, quiet enough that only the boy and the two soldiers beside him could hear.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "That's the part most men don't manage."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The boy's breathing steadied. Not calm -- but the breathing of someone who'd been seen by a man worth being seen by. Vale moved on.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The party split to assignments with the efficiency of people who'd learned to trust each other's competence even when they didn't trust each other.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik took the wall, tool-harness mostly empty but hands steady on his axes. He touched the loops where charges used to nest -- six left, maybe seven. He'd counted three times. The number didn't change, but the counting felt like work, and work was better than waiting.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Make them count,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he muttered to no one.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex ghosted to the tower stairs, testing her wrapped shoulder with a roll that pulled but held. Another week and she'd forget it had been cut. For now, she'd just have to be faster. She checked her daggers: three in the belt, two in the boots, one behind the collar. Enough. Never enough.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin held the center near the square, right arm bound tight under his sleeve. The scale pulsed coal-glow -- manageable, steady, the baseline hum of something awake and watching. He flexed his fingers. Pain sang through the lattice but didn't scream.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He could work with that. He'd been working with worse.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara took position at the gate, gloves immaculate, face calm as a closed book. Her fingers had stiffened through the day but still flexed when she needed them to. Still worked.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She pressed each tip against her thumb in sequence -- counting, always counting -- and decided they would be enough for tonight. Tomorrow was tomorrow's problem.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan moved to the bucket-line's edge, blades loose in their sheaths, shadow obedient at his boots in the lantern-glow. He stood with the perfect stillness of a man who was very deliberately not thinking about what his hands wanted to do, and the shadow -- for now -- agreed to be still.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls came on too quiet. Not the crash and charge of living attackers -- the sliding, wrongjoint approach of things that had forgotten how to announce themselves. The lantern-flames bent toward them before the shapes arrived, as if even light wanted to get a better look at what was coming.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The first bell went on ringing, because no one had told it to stop.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex met the first two in a blur -- dagger under ribs, second blade across the strange tendons behind a knee. The silhouettes buckled as anatomy demanded; their shadows whipped, failing to find purchase against Serana's ward-line.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The third came sideways, and she learned something: they adapted. The first two had attacked head-on. The third had watched the first two die and changed its angle. That was new. That was terrifying.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "They're learning!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she called to the line, rolling under a reaching limb that bent in directions limbs had no right to bend.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "They watch and adjust -- don't repeat patterns!"

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "On me!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana barked, stepping to take a rake meant for a baker at the bucket line.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her shield-arm shuddered with the impact -- hard, impossibly hard for something that weighed so little -- and something stirred at her shoulder -- habit, reflex, the light that used to answer her sword. It guttered before it could hold.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She put her body between the blow and the question and did not flinch.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The square was noise and shadow and the specific smell of fear-sweat mingling with the wrong-sweet corruption the thralls left on everything they touched. Cobblestones went slick with something that wasn't quite blood.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Torchlight carved the scene into slices: a baker swinging a stave with white-knuckled determination; two soldiers bracing each other back-to-back the way Vale had drilled them; Serana's shield catching lamplight like a small, defiant moon.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik thumbed a charge and skipped it off the cobbles; it caromed under three thralls and went off with a shuddering cough of light and sound. One collapsed. Two more staggered, smoking. The smell of ozone cut through the corruption-reek like a clean blade through fog.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Five left,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he grunted, tapping the emptied loop. Already counting. Already mourning.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan's blades worked short and economical, every cut a sentence with no wasted words.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: His shadow dragged at him slightly -- wanting, always wanting -- but he crushed it flat with jaw-locked effort and felt the warm trickle of a nosebleed vanish into the black of his collar. He didn't wipe it. Wiping meant acknowledging.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He cut, pivoted, cut again, and let the work carry him through the urge.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin kept center, throwing small heat -- lines on steel, a melted hinge, a slick made glassy at the right instant. Pain flared, then steadied. Manageable.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He could feel the scale pulling toward larger expenditure -- the way a river pulls toward a waterfall -- and held it back. Not yet. Save it. The night was young and the darkness was patient.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls retreated. Not fled -- retreated, the way a chess player pulls back a piece to reposition. They sank into the alleys they'd come from, and the silence they left was worse than the fighting because silence from things that didn't breathe was just them being quiet.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "They'll be back,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex said, dropping from the tower steps.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "And they'll remember everything we just showed them."

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Wonderful,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik said.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Enemies with a curriculum."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The second bell rang and the square was a box of breath and noise -- barrows shoved into a wall, barrels upright for the bucket chain, a slapped-together barricade that would break once, maybe twice.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Villagers braced spears with white knuckles; Vale ran the line, tapping shoulders, fixing spacing, making fear into formation.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Reserve on the right,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he told a sergeant.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "When the paladin gives you the nod, you move. No heroics. We live."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls came hard now, shadows flaring like oil under wind. More of them -- eight, ten, too fast to count -- and they came from three directions because they'd remembered where the defense was thin and aimed for it.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Thornik!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex called from the parapet.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Left!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He burned two charges to break a rush that would have folded the line. The belt went lighter at his ribs. He tapped the emptiness with a grim snort.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Three left. Then it's axes and prayer."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Prayer's out of stock,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana said through her teeth, driving a thrall back with the flat of her blade.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "You'll have to settle for geometry and stubbornness."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara was cool and surgical -- veils of suggestion and stutters of hesitation blooming in enemy minds, buying heartbeats where heartbeats meant survival.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls' coordination would fragment for a breath, two breaths, their learning interrupted by psychic static that Nyxara laid down with the precision of a woman placing caltrops. She did not reach for the deeper well yet. Not yet.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The patron's price was measured in fingertips, and she had a limited supply.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A scream cut through the chaos -- civilian down in the square, leg twisted wrong.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A voice cut through the noise -- not a shout, a command, the kind that had been obeyed on fields worse than this:

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Don't move him! Spine's compromised!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin turned.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A man knelt over the fallen villager -- grey-bearded, built like two decades of campaign work that had never quite softened, hands moving with the unhurried speed of someone who'd stopped being surprised by injury somewhere around middle age.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Battered medical bag, open on bloody cobbles. Leather strap between his teeth while he worked, because his hands were busy and his mouth had learned to hold things.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Every motion economical, precise -- not the careful slowness of someone trying to get it right, but the fluent speed of someone who'd gotten it right ten thousand times and stopped thinking about it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He didn't look up.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "You."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: This at a woman nearby.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Neck brace. My bag. Green strap."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then to the wounded man, in the gentler voice of a carpenter addressing a split beam:

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "You're going to live. I've pulled men through worse than this with less. The bad news is you're going to remember it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then to Caelin, still not looking up:

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Are you bleeding?"

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "No."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Then step aside. I have a limited amount of time and an unlimited amount of injured people."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin stepped aside. The man splinted, wrapped, assessed, and moved on with the relentless efficiency of a river that knows its course and doesn't ask permission.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Good news,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: the man told a screaming patient with a broken leg, already fitting a spear-shaft splint with someone's belt.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "You'll live. You'll walk with a limp that'll get you out of digging latrine pits for the rest of your natural life. In my experience that's a net positive."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin pushed harder. A spearline of red-gold leapt from his palm and cut a corridor through grasping shadows; a second cast flared broader, washing the cobbles clean for the bucket line to move. The lattice screamed inside his skin. He tasted copper.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The scale's coal-glow intensified to angry amber -- not the black-violet of corruption, but the white-edged heat of a system being pushed past design tolerance. He did it anyway, because three thralls had slipped the ward seam Vex was guarding.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She cut one's knee, hamstrung the second, rolled under the third and took its throat clean -- then a fourth she hadn't seen came in low. The butt of its spear caught her temple. The world went sideways.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Vex!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik shouted, trying to cut across, but the line surged to fill the gap and he was a beat too slow.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin turned and saw her folded at an angle wrong for living things.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Something in him broke open.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He screamed.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Fire answered.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Not careful. Not neat. A blast-front roared across half the square, a dragon's exhale made narrow by brutal will. Thralls blackened and curled like paper in a hearth. The heat washed over the barricade and set pitch-seams smoking.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Three seconds of absolute, obliterating power -- and then the lattice went dark, the filaments went cold, and Caelin dropped to his knees on scorched cobblestones with his arm dead from shoulder to fingertips and his vision going to snow.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The grey-bearded man was beside Vex in three strides. Hands on her neck, checking pulse. Fingers at her pupils. He looked up at Caelin with the measured assessment of a man who'd decided something about both of them.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "She'll live,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "You, on the other hand, need to stop doing that before you kill yourself."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls found the thin place at the well-head -- a junction where two ward-lines met at an awkward angle and the chalk had been scuffed by boot-traffic. Vale saw it before anyone else because Vale always saw it first.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He was already moving, three soldiers falling in behind him out of twelve years of habit.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They fought at the well-head with the compact efficiency of men defending a thing they knew they couldn't afford to lose. The water supply. The bucket chain's source. The difference between holding and burning.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale moved with no wasted motion, no glory -- the fighting of a man who had learned, over a career of border skirmishes and garrison work, that the loudest sword was usually the last one standing and the quietest one was usually the one that won.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He blocked a rake with his shield, pivoted his weight, and let the thrall's own momentum carry it onto his blade. His foot found the cobble-edge and used it for leverage. His breathing stayed steady.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The sixteen-year-old boy was on his left, spear braced the way Vale had squared it twenty minutes ago. The boy was scared -- his eyes too wide, his hands too white -- but the spear was level and his feet were set. He'd listened. That mattered more than bravery.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Three thralls pressed the well-head from the east, and Vale gave ground -- one step, two -- creating the angle he needed. His blade caught the first across the arm. The second he knocked into the well's stone lip, where it tangled on its own wrongjointed limbs. The third --

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The third came from the side.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A spike of shadow-metal, grown from the thrall's own arm like a thorn pushing through bark -- came in low and fast at an angle the boy couldn't have seen from his position.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale could see it because he was already looking, because he'd spent forty years looking at angles and knowing which ones killed.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He shoved the boy. Hard -- harder than he should have, hard enough to throw the kid three feet sideways and into the arms of a sergeant who caught him on reflex.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The spike took Vale through the ribs.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He went down on one knee, hand still on his sword, and stayed there for three heartbeats while his body argued with his decision. Then he looked at the sergeant.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Bucket chain's exposed,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Get two men on the south corner. Paladin takes command."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He coughed, and the sound had wet in it.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "And someone tell that boy -- "

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Another cough.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Tell him he did fine."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The grey-bearded surgeon was there in under a minute, bag open, hands moving before his knees had settled. He assessed the wound with the rapid competence of a man who'd seen this injury a hundred times and known the outcome of each. His hands slowed. Then stopped.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Captain,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said quietly.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I know,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale said.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I'm not stupid."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: His breathing had gone shallow. His hand was still on his sword.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "How long?"

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Minutes."

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Then I've got minutes."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale looked at the boy, who was standing ten feet away with a face that didn't understand why he was still standing.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Tell him what I said. About showing up."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The surgeon -- Aldric, Caelin would learn later -- nodded once.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "I'll tell him."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vale's hand relaxed on the sword. His eyes found the sky above the square, where smoke and stars competed for attention, and whatever he saw there was his. He breathed once more, shallow and careful, the breath of a man trying to make the last one count.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then he was gone, and the square was quieter for it -- not because the fighting had stopped, but because something fundamental about the defense had changed. The man who'd been telling Millbrook what to do for twelve years had stopped, and the town would have to remember how to stand on its own.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana saw it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The thralls had committed too far -- pushed too hard at the well-head, drawn too many of their shadows toward Vale's position. The left flank had thinned.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The ward-line there still held, its geometry intact, and the thralls pressing against it were strung out, uncoordinated, their stolen learning working against them because they'd learned to target strength and forgotten to guard their own weakness.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Now!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana shouted, and her voice carried the particular authority of someone who'd been taking orders from a god and had recently decided to take them from geometry instead.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her shield slammed into a leader's chest -- not the divine strike she'd once wielded, but muscle and mathematics, the physics of a heavy piece of metal moving very fast -- and the line stepped. The reserve flowed. The square held.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Her left hand came up instinctively, reaching for the wardlight that used to answer -- and for one blazing, impossible moment, it was there.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Silver-gold, bright as morning, pouring from her palm like something that had been held back and had finally, furiously, broken through. Not divine. Not the warm certainty of a god's answering hand.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Something rawer -- will made visible, fifteen years of discipline igniting on its own terms, answering its own prayer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: It lasted three seconds. The thralls nearest her buckled and writhed as if the light was acid. Two soldiers beside her gasped and fought harder. The ward-lines flared in sympathy.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then it was gone, and Serana stood with her hand still raised and her breath ragged and no idea what had just happened.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: And then -- like a thread cut -- the thralls began to break.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They did not run so much as fold and vanish, remnants sinking back into cracks they had no right to fit.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The shadows peeled off the cobblestones and seeped into mortar joints, into the spaces between roof-tiles, into the dark beneath the well's stone lip -- withdrawing with the obscene fluidity of things that had never been solid to begin with.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Silence arrived the way it always does after a fight that large: not as absence but as presence, a new thing settling over the square with weight.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The moaning and dripping and the meaningless roll of a loose bucket across cobblestones -- all of it still there, all of it somehow part of the silence rather than breaking it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The kind of quiet that means there is nothing left to kill and your body hasn't learned that yet.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin's arm was a column of white heat from shoulder to wrist. He became aware, slowly, that he was still holding a fighting stance and made himself lower it. The lattice ticked and began to cool by degrees. He looked down at his hand. Still there.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Still attached. That felt like more than he deserved.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik stood with an axe in each fist and his belt empty and blood on his beard that wasn't all his own. He ran his eyes across the square the way a man counts coin -- methodical, not hopeful. When he reached zero on the charge loops, he exhaled once through his nose. Then he put the axes away.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan went very still at the square's edge. His shadow lay flat and honest at his boots in the torchlight. He pressed the back of his wrist to his nose. Dry. He checked again. Habit. He exhaled once, slow, like a man easing a door closed so it didn't wake anyone.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex hadn't moved. She was breathing. Caelin could see it from here. That was what mattered.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The third bell rang once, useless, telling a town that already knew to stop fighting because there was nothing left to kill.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Smoke crawled along the square's roofline like a cat learning to be brave. The smell was sweat, pitch, cooked iron, and the bitter, wrong scent the thralls left behind -- the corruption-reek that clung to stone and cloth and memory and wouldn't wash out for days.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Water on the east thatch!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: a sergeant called -- Vale's echo in his voice, trying to fill a space that couldn't be filled.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: They moved bodies to the south lane and covered them with clean sheets that smelled of sun and soap.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Captain Vale lay among them, his hands crossed over the spear that had saved a boy who stood ten feet away and couldn't understand why he was still standing. The boy's spear was still level. His feet were still set.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd kept the form even after his reason for learning it had stopped breathing.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Serana knelt. Her light failed to gather. She prayed anyway, voice a rasp.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Guide him. He kept his watch."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The words landed because she said them, light or no. She'd stopped asking whether anyone was listening. She said them because they were true, and true was enough.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric worked in the center of the square, surrounded by wounded, hands moving with the mechanical rhythm of someone past exhaustion who has no intention of stopping. When Caelin approached, he didn't look up from wrapping a farmer's arm.

segments.speaker: thornik
segments.text: "Everyone needs a healer,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Get in line."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "She's still breathing,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin said quietly.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric's hands stilled for just a heartbeat.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Good. Keep it that way."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He tied off the bandage, moved to the next patient.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Wake me if she stops."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornik found the hinge at the east gate had splintered under blast and weight. No one asked. He set his hammer against the bent pin and began to work the metal back true. Tap, heat, wedge.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: His tool-harness hung empty at his ribs, loops that should have held cleverness now just leather and air. He worked anyway, the old way, with sweat and stubbornness. The gate would hold or it wouldn't, but it wouldn't fail because a dwarf had let it.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nyxara did not linger in the square once the dead were counted and the living were moving. She took a narrow stair to the little room behind the archive, slid the bolt, and sat very still until the tremor in her hands stopped.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The gloves would come off later, in private. The accounting would be done where no one could see.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Durgan cleaned his blades with method that could have been prayer. His shadow lay obedient at his boots; his shoulders were too square.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: When the nosebleed started again, he pressed the heel of his hand to it and turned slightly so the dark cloth drank the red. He kept his eyes on Caelin across the square and told himself that standing here was still a choice he was making.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin found Aldric on an upturned bucket at the square's edge, stitching a cut in his own forearm with the calm of a man mending a saddle.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You do that yourself?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin asked.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Every time."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric bit the thread.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Forty years of practice. You get good at it or you get dead, and I've always had a mild preference for the first option."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin crouched.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "You're not from Millbrook."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "No."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric rolled his sleeve down.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Third Harrow Company, retired. Field surgeon fifteen years, then walking between towns for another ten patching whoever needed patching."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He paused, looking at his hands -- steady, clean, the hands of a man who'd spent a career making sure they stayed both.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I was attached to the garrison at Veln's Post when the seals started failing two years ago. Forty men. Good men, most of them -- well-trained, sensible, the sort who ate their vegetables and didn't gamble more than they could lose."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He said it with the flat affect of someone who'd had time to turn the memory into something he could carry without breaking.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Thirty-seven of them died to something that came through the lower vault. I couldn't cut it out. Couldn't bandage it. Couldn't do a single useful thing except count the living afterward and try not to notice how short the count was."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You survived."

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "I was in the east tower when it happened. A healer who was somewhere else when people needed healing."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He looked at his hands once, briefly, then didn't again.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Since then I've been trying to get back into the plateau's border country. Find out what came through that vault. Find out if the other sealed vaults are next. Because whatever killed those thirty-seven men is going to do it again, and I'd like to be in the room this time. Not because I think I can stop it. But because I owe it the courtesy of being present when it tries."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin said, carefully:

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Veln's Post is Depthspire's eastern approach."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I know where it is."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A beat.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "We're going in. North, toward Depthspire."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I know what you are."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric nodded at Caelin's wrapped arm.

segments.speaker: serana
segments.text: "Saw the mark when you were on your knees beside the woman. Knew what you were about."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stood, joints filing their usual complaints.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You'll need a healer. That plateau has spent the last two years demonstrating that it can open people up in ways that don't respond well to wishful thinking."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Sounds like a reason to stay away,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex said from behind Caelin. He hadn't heard her cross the square -- which meant she was either getting better at moving quietly or his hearing was getting worse, and neither option was comforting.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "That's what the thirty-seven thought,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Aldric said pleasantly,

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "before they stopped having opinions."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He looked at Vex's shoulder with the eyes of a man who'd assessed a great many wounds and was unimpressed by what he was seeing.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "That wrap needs redoing. Sit down."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex sat down. It was the most decisive thing she'd done all night.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She woke near midnight. Caelin was there, which she registered before she registered anything else.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Six,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said, when her eyes focused.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "Before you fell. In case you were counting."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Seven,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "You missed one at the drainpipe."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He almost smiled.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "How's your head?"

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "Feels like someone rang it like a bell."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She shifted, winced, settled.

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Vale?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The almost-smile went away.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "He saved people. We'll say his name right."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She was quiet a moment. Then:

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "This Aldric. He any good?"

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "He stitched his own arm without flinching and told a man with a broken leg that it was a net positive. I think he'll do."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "Mm."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: She closed her eyes.

segments.speaker: aldric
segments.text: "Don't let him touch my shoulder again while I'm awake. He's not gentle."

segments.speaker: vex
segments.text: "He said you'd say that. He said if you say that, he was right about the wrap and you can thank him later."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Vex said something short and accurate about Aldric's bedside manner and went back to sleep.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Night came early, as it does after battles. Millbrook slept in ragged shifts.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin sat outside on the stable step and watched the sky find stars one at a time.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The scale had settled lower than it had been in days -- the scream-cast had burned something down to a truer level, cleared some pressure he hadn't known had been building. He flexed his fingers in the dark.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The lattice answered steady and sullen, like a fire that had stopped trying to be impressive and committed to just burning.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He looked north. The scale warmed a fraction -- the same direction it had been pointing since the mountain, patient as a compass needle. Somewhere out there the Depthspire aurora had written curtains across the sky.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Somewhere out there L's note said two weeks. Somewhere out there thirty-seven men from Veln's Post were owed an answer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The third bell had rung too late. Millbrook held, but Vale was gone, and the cost of holding felt heavier than the victory.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He let that sit. He wasn't going to argue with it. He let it settle beside the other things he was carrying, and then he went in to sleep in pieces, the way you do after battles, and let the north wait until morning.
